facade_colour: white-painted glazed storefront with multicolour mural surround beneath a beige block-clad upper facade

hcd_data.statement_of_contribution: Each property has value as a late-nineteenth- to early-twentieth-century House-form building that reflects vernacular interpretations of Victorian and early-twentieth-century architectural styles. Through its construction date and typology, each property is associated with the District’s early residential development from the 1850s to the early 1900s. Constructed as a semi-detached pair, they retain most of their original building features including shared cross-gabled roofline, fenestration pattern, second storey oriel window, and brick cladding with stone banding. Each property has contextual value because it supports and maintains the historic mixed use character of the area and is physically, functionally, visually, and historically linked to the surrounding market area.

facade_description: 323 Augusta Ave presents a house-form, semi-detached facade within the Kensington Market HCD. The street elevation spans roughly 5.2 metres across 3 storeys and is capped by a gable, consistent with its house-form, semi-detached typology. The facade reads primarily as mixed in a white-painted glazed storefront with multicolour mural surround beneath a beige block-clad upper facade palette, laid in running bond with light grey mortar joints. The facade keeps a domestic opening rhythm of 1, 2, 2 window bays from lower to upper levels, using mixed, and 1 principal entrance. Character-defining facade elements include bay window, decorative brick, cornice. HCD note: Each property has value as a late-nineteenth- to early-twentieth-century House-form building that reflects vernacular interpretations of Victorian and early-twentieth-century architectural styles. Through its construction date and typology, each property is associated with the District’s early residential development from the 1850s to the early 1900s. Constructed as a semi-detached pair, they retain most of their original building features including shared cross-gabled roofline, fenestration pattern, second storey oriel window, and brick cladding with stone banding. Each property has contextual value because it supports and maintains the historic mixed use character of the area and is physically, functionally, visually, and historically linked to the surrounding market area.
